channel.title: Event Security on Close Protection and Security Services | CloseProtectionHire.com
channel.description: Recent content in Event Security on Close Protection and Security Services | CloseProtectionHire.com

item.title: Event Security in Aarhus
item.description: Aarhus doesn’t have one dominant conference venue the way Copenhagen has; instead, its event calendar tracks its major employers. Maersk, Vestas, Arla Foods, Salling Group and Jysk each run their own briefings, product launches and stakeholder events through the year, and Aarhus University adds a genuine academic conference circuit on top, drawing on more than 44,000 students and a research base centred on the Katrinebjerg IT cluster and the Agro Food Park life-sciences campus. That spread across employer, university and life-sciences venues means an event security plan for Aarhus is built site by site rather than around a single exhibition hall.

item.title: Event Security in Charleroi
item.description: Charleroi’s event security brief splits cleanly in two, and the split matters more here than in most Belgian cities. Gosselies, a district north of the centre, hosts Sonaca’s aerospace-structures operations and the A6K digital-innovation hub, funded through the EU Recovery and Resilience Plan and set to scale from around 1,000 to 10,000 learners a year from autumn 2026; both sit on modern campuses that plan much like any purpose-built corporate site. The wider city centre, postal code 6000, carries a different reputation entirely, a legacy of Charleroi’s coal and steel past and regional unemployment that stood at 7.8% in Wallonia in 2025 against a national rate of 6.2%.

item.title: Event Security in Cordoba
item.description: Cordoba’s event calendar runs on two very different registers in the same month each May. The Patios Festival, Fiesta de los Patios de Cordoba, recognised by UNESCO as Intangible Cultural Heritage since December 2012, opens private courtyards across five old-town zones and pulls dense crowds into pedestrianised streets that were laid out centuries before crowd control was a planning discipline. The Feria de Cordoba, the city’s May fair, runs at the same time at the El Arenal fairground on the Guadalquivir, a single-site event with a far more conventional gate-and-perimeter brief. Planning for one doesn’t transfer cleanly to the other.

item.title: Event Security in Gdansk
item.description: Gdansk’s most significant event venue carries history most cities can’t match. The European Solidarity Centre stands beside the shipyard gates where the strike that began on 14 August 1980 led to the Gdansk Agreement, signed 31 August 1980 by Lech Walesa and Deputy Prime Minister Mieczyslaw Jagielski, and to the Solidarity trade union’s formal registration that November. Institutional and commemorative events at the Centre carry real weight, and any booking near the mid-August anniversary period benefits from checking the calendar first, since official visits and elevated security presence are a genuine possibility around that date.

item.title: Event Security in Hannover
item.description: Ask anyone in industrial manufacturing what Hannover means and the answer is Hannover Messe: widely described in that sector as the world’s largest industrial trade fair, run by Deutsche Messe AG each April on grounds that rank among the largest exhibition sites anywhere, with a typical edition drawing more than 4,000 exhibiting companies from over 70 countries. Fair week reshapes the whole city, hotel rooms disappear, transfers slow down, and any corporate hospitality event booked the same week needs its own contingency built in from the start rather than added once the problem shows up.

item.title: Event Security in Katowice
item.description: Katowice’s event security brief is set almost entirely by one venue. The Katowice International Congress Centre, MCK, and the adjacent Spodek Arena hosted COP24, the UN Climate Change Conference, from 2 to 14 December 2018, drawing roughly 20,000 attendees from 190 countries with Poland’s climate minister Michal Kurtyka as COP president, and the venue continues to host conferences and institutional events on a comparable scale. Any booking there of real size benefits from the same advance security survey a gathering that large demands, mapping entry points and attendee flow before the doors open.

item.title: Event Security in Namur
item.description: Namur carries an institutional weight its size doesn’t immediately suggest. It has been the official capital of Wallonia since 1986, confirmed in 2010, and hosts both the Parliament of Wallonia and the Government of Wallonia at the Elysette, which means government and institutional events here run on their own protocol tied to sitting-week schedules rather than a general public-events calendar. Access arrangements and any additional security presence around the government quarter get confirmed through the host institution well ahead of the date, not assumed from prior bookings.

item.title: Event Security in Nuremberg
item.description: NürnbergMesse is the single fact that shapes most event security planning in this city: one of the world’s largest exhibition venues, hosting fairs whose scale most cities never see. Spielwarenmesse alone, by the organiser’s own account the world’s biggest toy trade fair, draws around 2,800 exhibitors from some 60 countries and roughly 67,000 trade visitors a year, typically in late January or early February. BrauBeviale, running since 1978, and Consumenta add further fair-week peaks through the calendar, and each one brings its own exhibitor badge system that a security plan has to work with rather than around.

item.title: Event Security in Rouen
item.description: Rouen sits in an unusual position for a French regional capital. French Ministry of Interior figures from April 2024 ranked it 4th of 368 French cities with more than 22,500 residents for recorded crime per capita, 11,598 offences against a population of roughly 114,000 in 2023, ahead of Lyon and Paris, a statistic the Ministry itself frames as informational rather than a formal risk index. For event security planning, it still means a tighter perimeter and escort brief than most comparable French bookings, applied to a city that otherwise carries the institutional weight of being capital of Normandy and prefecture of Seine-Maritime.

item.title: Event Security in Wroclaw
item.description: Wroclaw’s event calendar runs on the strength of its business-services sector rather than any single flagship venue. KPMG Poland counted 234 modern business-service centres operating in the city as of May 2025, with named operations including Google, HP, Nokia, IBM, UBS and Volvo IT, and that density drives a steady stream of corporate conferences, product launches and hospitality events concentrated around the Bielany Wroclawskie business-park corridor, a genuinely different environment from the tourist-focused Rynek old town a few kilometres away.

item.title: Event Security in Antwerp
item.description: Antwerp’s event work rarely looks like a standard conference booking. Much of it runs through the diamond trade, where the World Diamond Centre operates its own access rules independently of the city around it, and through the petrochemical and logistics conferences generated by Port of Antwerp-Bruges, one of Europe’s largest chemical clusters. A smaller share is ordinary corporate hospitality in the city centre. Each of these settings demands a different access plan, so no two Antwerp bookings look quite alike.

item.title: Event Security in Bremen
item.description: Bremen does not have the profile of Germany’s larger conference cities, and that is precisely what makes its event calendar distinctive: corporate work here clusters around Airbus’s aerospace site, the twin-port relationship with Bremerhaven, and the converted-warehouse conference venues of Uberseestadt, rather than around a single flagship exhibition centre. A delegation visiting for an Airbus briefing has a very different security profile from one attending a maritime-sector conference in a hotel ballroom, even though both are booked as “corporate events.”

item.title: Event Security in Dusseldorf
item.description: Dusseldorf’s event security work is dominated by one institution: Messe Dusseldorf, which runs more than 50 trade fairs a year and turns the city into a rotating series of specialist worlds, boat buyers one month, printing-industry executives the next, wine importers after that. Each fair brings its own crowd, its own confidentiality concerns, and its own idea of what a “delegate” looks like, so treating them as interchangeable would be a mistake we do not make.

item.title: Event Security in Leipzig
item.description: Few cities pair a medieval trade fair heritage with a modern automotive manufacturing base quite like Leipzig does. Leipziger Messe has run exhibitions since 1165, and its calendar today spans the Leipzig Book Fair on one end and heavy industrial exhibitions on the other, while BMW Group Plant Leipzig and Porsche Leipzig generate a steady stream of corporate site-visit events entirely separate from the Messe grounds. That combination means our planning has to flex between a public-facing publishing event and a credentialed factory tour within the same week, sometimes within the same delegation’s itinerary.

item.title: Event Security in Liege
item.description: Liege’s event security work sits at an odd intersection: a riverside conference venue in the Palais des Congres, and a logistics and e-commerce sector driven by an airport that moves more cargo than passengers. Liege Airport ranks as the fifth-largest cargo hub in Europe by volume, running around the clock with ASL Airlines Belgium based there, which means the conferences it generates often gather at venues near the airport rather than downtown. It is a different kind of event city from its Belgian neighbours, and the security plan has to reflect that.

item.title: Event Security in Lille
item.description: Lille runs two very different kinds of event in parallel: the large-scale conference and exhibition trade at Lille Grand Palais, a venue built to seat up to 20,000 delegates, and the smaller, more discreet corporate-campus work generated by Euralille’s roughly 150 resident companies and by retail and logistics giants such as Auchan and Decathlon, both headquartered nearby. Planning for one does not transfer neatly to the other; a Grand Palais exhibition needs multiple checkpoints and entrance streams, while a supplier day at a corporate campus needs a security plan that fits around the host firm’s own access rules.

item.title: Event Security in Montpellier
item.description: Montpellier’s event calendar has two distinct centres of gravity: the Corum, a conventional congress venue a short walk from the old town, and a growing technology and life sciences sector built on French Tech Capital status since 2019, Sanofi’s long-standing presence, and IBM’s site, which has operated in the city since 1965. A pitch event for local founders and a Sanofi partnership conference draw very different crowds, and our security plans reflect that rather than defaulting to one standard approach.

item.title: Event Security in Palma de Mallorca
item.description: Palma de Mallorca’s event work does not look like a conventional conference city’s, and it should not be planned as one. The bookings that come our way here are private villa functions, superyacht arrival events at Real Club Nautico de Palma or STP Shipyard Palma, and corporate or HNWI gatherings up at Son Vida, all of which call for a different kind of discretion than a credentialed trade fair does.

item.title: Event Security in Strasbourg
item.description: Strasbourg carries a security profile unlike any other city in this network, shaped by two separate facts that rarely apply together: it hosts European Parliament plenary sessions roughly monthly, and on 11 December 2018 it was the site of a terrorist attack on its Christmas market that killed five people and wounded eleven before the gunman was killed by police on 13 December, following a manhunt involving around 700 officers. Both facts, one institutional and recurring, one historical and singular, shape how every event here gets planned.

item.title: Event Security in Stuttgart
item.description: Stuttgart’s event security work is shaped by three things that do not usually sit together in one city: a trade fair venue built almost on top of the airport, two of the world’s best-known automotive manufacturers headquartered locally, and a folk festival calendar rivalling Oktoberfest in scale. Messe Stuttgart’s fairs, AMB and Blechexpo among them, benefit from a roughly 12km, direct route from Stuttgart Airport, while Mercedes-Benz at Untertürkheim and Porsche at Zuffenhausen generate corporate visits that call for a much quieter, more discreet security posture entirely.

item.title: Event Security in Bologna
item.description: Bologna’s event calendar runs on trade fairs and pharma-sector conferences more than tourism, and that shapes what event security actually looks like here. BolognaFiere alone carries EIMA, Cersaie, and a historic Motor Show through the year, each with its own hall layout, exhibitor credentials, and vehicle-staging needs. Add a pharmaceutical and biotech cluster built around firms like IMA Group and Dompé, and the result is a city where discreet executive cover and large-scale trade-fair crowd management are both regular requirements, often in the same week.

item.title: Event Security in Bordeaux
item.description: Bordeaux’s event security work runs on two very different sectors that rarely overlap on the same day. The wine trade brings Vinexpo and a circuit of tastings and receptions across the Medoc, Pauillac, and Saint-Emilion chateaux, where high-value stock and a rural setting call for a quieter, largely static approach. The aerospace and defence cluster around Merignac and Bordeaux-Lac, anchored by Dassault Aviation, Thales, and MBDA, generates a separate calendar of investor briefings with a more conventional, access-controlled profile. In the city centre, Place de la Bourse and the Miroir d’Eau remain the default venue for corporate receptions and civic functions.

item.title: Event Security in Eindhoven
item.description: Eindhoven’s event calendar is shaped by two forces: Dutch Design Week’s public-facing crowds at Strijp-S and the Philips Innovation Campus, and the far more discreet world of semiconductor-sector investor days tied to ASML in neighbouring Veldhoven. The two could not be more different in profile. Design Week needs conventional crowd management across converted industrial buildings, keeping general admission separate from trade and press lanes; ASML’s investor events need almost the opposite, a quiet, tightly verified guest list rather than a large public footprint.

item.title: Event Security in Genoa
item.description: Genoa’s event security work is shaped by two things most Italian cities do not have: a working port and a medieval alley network that cars cannot enter. Porto Antico and the Stazione Marittima bring cruise arrivals and maritime-industry conferences tied to Fincantieri, Grimaldi Lines, and the wider shipping sector, while the Caruggi’s narrow lanes mean delegate movement near the old town is planned on foot rather than car to door. Palazzo Ducale on Piazza de Ferrari remains the go-to venue for corporate receptions and civic functions, and its grand internal layout needs the same room-by-room access mapping as any heritage building pressed into event use.

item.title: Event Security in Ghent
item.description: Ghent’s event calendar spans two quite different settings: Flanders Expo, one of Belgium’s principal trade and exhibition venues in Sint-Denijs-Westrem, and the Ghent-Terneuzen Canal industrial zone, home to ArcelorMittal’s steel operations, Volvo Cars Belgium, and pharmaceutical firms including UCB and the Ablynx and Sanofi site. The exhibition venue needs conventional, multi-day crowd and access management; the industrial visits typically sit inside a host site’s own security arrangements, with our teams coordinating delegate movement and close protection alongside site security rather than building an independent perimeter.

item.title: Event Security in Malaga
item.description: Malaga’s event calendar splits fairly cleanly into two worlds: the tech-sector conferences at Malaga TechPark, where firms including Vodafone, Google, and Amazon maintain regional offices, and the private-event circuit along the Costa del Sol toward Marbella, sixty kilometres west via the AP-7. The first calls for conventional corporate access control across a multi-building campus; the second calls for the kind of low-profile static presence that suits a villa reception or gallery preview rather than a badge-checked lobby. Handling both well means briefing officers differently within the same week, not applying one template to every booking.

item.title: Event Security in Nantes
item.description: Nantes brings together aerospace, maritime energy, and a growing technology scene, and each generates its own kind of event security requirement. Airbus and Thales facilities at Bouguenais run conventional, access-controlled corporate conferences and investor briefings, while Chantiers de l’Atlantique in Saint-Nazaire, some 60km west via the N165, hosts shipyard-scale events including major cruise-ship launches that draw large public crowds around an active industrial site. The Ile de Nantes business district adds a third, newer strand of technology and corporate events across a mix of converted and purpose-built venues.

item.title: Event Security in Rennes
item.description: Rennes runs two very different kinds of event, and both shape our planning here. The Beaulieu and Atalante tech campuses, home to Orange, Thales, and Technicolor facilities, generate a steady calendar of investor days and technology conferences that call for conventional corporate access control. Place de la Republique and Place du Parlement, by contrast, serve as both the city’s principal civic-event venue and its recurring protest-assembly point, which means any booking there needs a check of the public-order calendar alongside the usual venue survey.

item.title: Event Security in Utrecht
item.description: Utrecht’s event security work is defined by proximity: Jaarbeurs, one of the Netherlands’ principal exhibition venues, sits directly beside Utrecht Centraal, the busiest railway station in the country, so any large exhibition there means planning around a permanent baseline of station traffic rather than a clean, self-contained venue. The city’s financial and insurance sector, headquartered around Rabobank, a.s.r., and Triodos Bank, adds a second, quieter calendar of corporate conferences and investor briefings that call for a more conventional, discreet security profile.

item.title: Event Security in Zaragoza
item.description: Zaragoza’s event security work is built around trade fairs and industry rather than tourism. Feria de Zaragoza runs multi-day sector exhibitions across a multi-hall site on the city’s eastern edge, PLAZA, one of Europe’s largest logistics platforms, hosts investor and trade events in a working industrial setting, and the Stellantis plant at Figueruelas, some 40km northwest, draws corporate and government delegations for factory visits. None of these are conventional hotel-conference bookings, so the access-control and delegate-movement planning differs from venue to venue rather than following a single template.

item.title: Event Security in Bilbao
item.description: Bilbao has transformed from an industrial port city into one of Spain’s most internationally recognised event destinations, with the Guggenheim Museum having anchored a broader regeneration that now includes world-class conference infrastructure at BEC and Palacio Euskalduna. The city’s corporate sector is defined by two global-scale companies – Iberdrola and BBVA – whose event programmes generate a consistent flow of high-profile energy and financial sector gatherings. Effective event security in Bilbao requires familiarity with a feature not found in most Spanish cities: the Ertzaintza, the Basque Autonomous Police, which operates as the primary law enforcement body in the Basque Country and has its own coordination protocols for event security that differ from those that apply elsewhere in Spain. Our teams brief on these protocols before every engagement. For the broader risk picture, see our Bilbao city page.

item.title: Event Security in Bridgetown
item.description: Bridgetown is the capital of Barbados and one of the Caribbean’s most significant locations for high-protocol diplomatic and institutional events. The Caribbean Development Bank, headquartered in Wildey on the city’s outskirts, anchors an annual calendar of multilateral meetings; CARICOM Heads of Government summits have been held in Bridgetown multiple times; and the Barbados International Business Summit draws offshore financial services leaders from across the Atlantic. FCDO Barbados travel advice (2024) rates the island as generally low risk, a favourable rating that reflects both the security environment and the strength of governance institutions including the Royal Barbados Police Force. The Bridgetown city guide provides full background on the city’s security context.

item.title: Event Security in Dammam
item.description: Dammam is the administrative capital of Saudi Arabia’s Eastern Province and the centre of the Kingdom’s petroleum industry, home to Saudi Aramco’s headquarters in Dhahran and a dense cluster of oil, gas and petrochemical industry infrastructure. The city’s MICE calendar reflects this industrial base: oil sector technical symposia, SAIBEX at Dhahran Expo, and Vision 2030 investment forums are the anchor events, supplemented by contractor briefings at Al Khobar hotels and strategic summits at Aramco-affiliated venues. FCDO Saudi Arabia travel advice (2024) maintains an elevated terrorism warning with specific reference to the Eastern Province’s oil infrastructure risk. The Dammam city guide provides a comprehensive security overview.

item.title: Event Security in Florence
item.description: Florence is one of Europe’s most distinctive event cities, combining world-class conference infrastructure with a concentration of historic palaces and cultural institutions that attract fashion, art market, and luxury brand events of international significance. Effective event security in Florence requires familiarity with the specific operating conditions of heritage venues, the credential structures of trade shows such as Pitti Uomo, and the public-order landscape of a city centre that experiences significant pedestrian congestion during major event weeks. Our teams are briefed on all of these before the first delegate arrives. For background on the city’s general risk environment, see our Florence city page.

item.title: Event Security in Izmir
item.description: Izmir is Turkey’s third-largest city and principal Aegean port, with a commercial event calendar that includes the Izmir International Fair (one of Turkey’s longest-running trade exhibitions, active for over 90 years at the Kulturpark grounds in Alsancak), the Izmir Economic Forum, and a busy maritime and logistics sector conference programme. FCDO Turkey travel advice (2024) maintains an elevated terrorism warning for Turkey; Izmir has experienced a serious attack (January 2021 court complex bombing) and carries a moderate risk rating that requires more detailed security planning than a typical Western European event city. Full background on Izmir’s security environment is set out on the Izmir city guide.

item.title: Event Security in Las Palmas
item.description: Las Palmas de Gran Canaria is the commercial and administrative capital of the Canary Islands, combining a significant cruise port, a growing MICE sector and a year-round calendar of trade events across maritime, tourism and energy industries. The city’s position as a mid-Atlantic waypoint gives it an international delegate profile that reaches beyond Spain: shipping companies, cruise lines and energy firms regularly bring senior personnel through the city for operational meetings and sector conferences. Security planning for these events draws on FCDO Spain travel advice (2024), which rates the Canary Islands as low risk overall, with petty theft in tourist and port areas as the primary concern for visiting business delegates. Coordination with the Las Palmas city security infrastructure is a standard part of every event plan we deliver.

item.title: Event Security in Male
item.description: Male is the capital and commercial centre of the Maldives, a nation defined by its Indian Ocean geography and its role as a hub for regional diplomatic, environmental and blue-economy discussions. The city’s extreme density, island geography and limited land area create an event security environment that is unlike most capital cities: foot-based escort, speedboat transfers and multi-island logistics are standard features rather than exceptions. FCDO Maldives travel advice (2024) identifies a terrorism threat from documented local extremist networks, gang-related crime in residential districts and political instability following the 2023 elections as the primary risk factors for visiting delegates. The full security context is covered on the Male city guide.

item.title: Event Security in Maracaibo
item.description: Maracaibo is Venezuela’s second city and the centre of the country’s petroleum industry, located on the western shore of Lago Maracaibo in Zulia State. It presents one of the most demanding security environments for international business travel in the Western Hemisphere. FCDO Venezuela travel advice (2024) advises against all but essential travel, citing very high violent crime, kidnapping, armed robbery and significant territorial control by organised criminal groups including colectivos. This is not a city where conventional open-access events can currently be planned. The security assessment for Maracaibo in detail is covered on the Maracaibo city guide.

item.title: Event Security in Medina
item.description: Medina is one of Islam’s two holiest cities, a status that shapes every aspect of event planning within its boundaries. Non-Muslim delegates face a formal access restriction to the Haram district surrounding the Prophet’s Mosque, enforced by Saudi authorities. Business events take place in hotel conference facilities outside the Haram boundary, with the Anwar Al Madinah Mövenpick, the Oberoi Medina and the Crowne Plaza Medina the primary venues. FCDO Saudi Arabia travel advice (2024) maintains an elevated terrorism warning nationally and notes the strict social law environment, which is more closely enforced in Medina than in most Saudi cities. The Medina city guide covers the city’s security picture in full.

item.title: Event Security in Nassau
item.description: Nassau is the capital of the Bahamas and the Caribbean’s most active location for major conferences, regional summits and high-value corporate events. The Baha Mar complex on Cable Beach and Atlantis Paradise Island together provide convention capacity that places Nassau ahead of most Caribbean competitors for large-scale event hosting, and the city’s status as the Bahamas’ commercial hub draws a dense calendar of financial services, tourism industry, investment and CARICOM political events throughout the year. FCDO Bahamas travel advice (2024) and the US State Dept Level 2 advisory (2024) both identify violent crime as the primary security concern, with detailed risk mapping essential for delegate movement planning. The Nassau city guide provides the full security context.

item.title: Event Security in Nice
item.description: The Cote d’Azur is one of the most demanding event security environments in Europe. The concentration of HNWI principals, celebrities, luxury brands, and high-value physical assets – combined with the elevated terrorism posture under Vigipirate Urgence Attentat and the specific context of the 2016 Nice attack – means that security planning here carries a level of complexity and consequence that exceeds that of most comparable European event cities. Our teams are briefed on all of these dimensions before any engagement, and the 72-hour brief is treated as a minimum rather than a ceiling for intelligence preparation. For the broader risk picture for Nice and the Alpes-Maritimes, see our Nice city page.

item.title: Event Security in Palermo
item.description: Palermo presents a distinctive event security challenge among Italian cities. Its combination of substantive agrifood, energy, and cultural events with a risk environment that ISTAT 2022 crime data characterise as above-average versus Italian national figures – together with the specific due-diligence context provided by the DIA 2023 annual report on organised crime in Sicily – requires a more thorough pre-event assessment and contractor vetting process than would typically be necessary in Milan, Rome, or Florence. None of this prevents effective event delivery in Palermo; it means that the planning stage must include elements that may be omitted in lower-complexity Italian cities. Our teams are briefed on all relevant context before each engagement. For the city’s general risk profile, see our Palermo city page.

item.title: Event Security in Rotterdam
item.description: Rotterdam is northern Europe’s most significant maritime and port event city, hosting Europort – one of the largest sector trade shows on the continent – alongside a year-round calendar of maritime arbitration proceedings, energy infrastructure events, and port-sector conferences. The security environment here is shaped by the industrial character of the city: Europe’s largest port brings a specific context of logistics and commodity trade that creates background conditions distinct from those of a conventional conference city. Our teams brief on this context and on the specific operating requirements of Rotterdam’s principal venues, from the large-format Rotterdam Ahoy complex to the more intimate waterfront settings of the Cruise Terminal and Wilhelminapier. For the city’s broader risk picture, see our Rotterdam city page.

item.title: Event Security in Seville
item.description: Seville is Andalusia’s foremost event city, combining a major purpose-built convention centre on Isla de la Cartuja with a concentration of historic palatial venues that attract high-protocol corporate and institutional events. The city’s economy is anchored in energy, aerospace, and agribusiness – sectors that generate a consistent programme of conferences, investor days, and sector summits throughout the year. Effective event security in Seville requires familiarity with both the standard operating conditions of FIBES and the significantly more complex conditions created by Semana Santa and Feria de Abril, which can transform the access environment across the entire city centre for weeks at a time. For the broader risk picture, see our Seville city page.

item.title: Event Security in Tenerife
item.description: Tenerife is the largest of the Canary Islands and the most developed for corporate events and MICE travel, with a dual-zone geography that separates the government and congress-focused capital Santa Cruz de Tenerife from the luxury resort and incentive cluster centred on Costa Adeje and Playa de las Americas in the south. This geographic division shapes every aspect of event security planning: venue selection, airport assignment, transfer routing and inter-resort movement for multi-day programmes must all be addressed before the first delegate arrives. FCDO Spain travel advice (2024) rates the island as low risk, with petty theft in tourist areas the primary concern. Full background on the island’s security environment is available on the Tenerife city guide.

item.title: Event Security in The Hague
item.description: The Hague occupies a position in the international event calendar that no other European city replicates. As the seat of the International Criminal Court, the International Court of Justice, the OPCW, Europol, and INTERPOL, it hosts a continuous programme of proceedings, diplomatic conferences, and international NGO summits that bring together some of the most high-profile and, in some cases, specifically targeted individuals in the world of international law and diplomacy. Effective event security here requires understanding not only the standard conference security framework but also the specific protocols that govern operations near and within international institution premises, and the threat assessment considerations that apply to principals connected to active ICC or ICJ proceedings. For the city’s general risk environment, see our The Hague city page.

item.title: Event Security in Thessaloniki
item.description: Thessaloniki is Greece’s second city and the commercial capital of northern Greece, with a strong MICE calendar anchored by the Thessaloniki International Trade Fair and supplemented by internationally recognised cultural festivals including the Thessaloniki International Film Festival and the Documentary Festival. The TIF’s position as a major Balkan trade event, combined with its long history as a venue for political as well as commercial activity, creates a security environment that is more complex than Thessaloniki’s overall low-moderate risk rating might suggest. FCDO Greece travel advice (2024) specifically calls out demonstrations, football-related disorder and petty crime as areas for awareness. Full background on the city’s security picture is available on the Thessaloniki city guide.

item.title: Event Security in Toulouse
item.description: Toulouse holds a unique position in the European event calendar: it is simultaneously a world-class aerospace industrial hub and a university city with an active public political culture. This combination means that event security planning here must address both the controlled, credential-heavy environment of aerospace sector gatherings and the somewhat less predictable conditions created by periodic civil unrest and the Vigipirate Urgence Attentat national terrorism posture. Both dimensions are built into every security plan we deliver for Toulouse events. For the general risk environment and city context, see our Toulouse city page.

item.title: Event Security in Turin
item.description: Turin occupies a distinctive position in the Italian event calendar. Its industrial heritage sustains a major conference scene across automotive, aerospace, and food sectors, while OGR Torino and Lingotto Fiere have established themselves as venues capable of hosting international events at the highest level. Security planning in Turin must account for two features that distinguish it from other Italian cities: the heightened frequency of politically motivated demonstrations, and the specific operating requirements of heritage and industrial-converted venues where access control points are not always where a conventional venue plan would place them. Our teams brief extensively on both before each event. For the general risk environment, see our Turin city page.

item.title: Event Security in Verona
item.description: Verona is one of Italy’s most distinctive event cities, defined by two overlapping calendar events that together cover much of the spring, summer, and autumn: Vinitaly, the world-class wine trade fair held at Veronafiere each April, and the Arena di Verona opera season, which runs from June through September and attracts international corporate hospitality events of the highest level. Security planning in Verona must account for the very different crowd and venue characteristics of these two environments – the high-throughput, multi-pavilion exhibition centre at Veronafiere and the ancient open-air amphitheatre in the historic centre – as well as the steady stream of wine-sector and luxury industry events that continue throughout the year. For the city’s general risk profile, see our Verona city page.

item.title: Event Security in Astana
item.description: Event security in Astana operates at the intersection of a capital city’s diplomatic event calendar and the specific risk environment of Kazakhstan’s energy sector and government institutions. The low-moderate risk environment (FCDO Kazakhstan, 2024) is shaped by the January 2022 civil unrest, ongoing counter-intelligence considerations for oil and gas sector delegates, and the logistical demands of extreme continental winters. All event security personnel are licensed under the Ministry of Internal Affairs (MVD), and large events with foreign participants require advance coordination through MVD-registered operators.

item.title: Event Security in Barranquilla
item.description:
Barranquilla event security covers Colombia’s Caribbean coast commercial hub, where an active corporate events market operates alongside a high-crime environment that requires professional security provision. SVSP-licensed operators, managed event transport, and close protection for senior principals are the foundations of professional event security in Atlantico.

For the full Barranquilla security picture, see the Barranquilla city briefing and secure airport transfers in Barranquilla for Ernesto Cortissoz Airport delegate transport.

item.title: Event Security in Basra
item.description: Event security in Basra operates in one of the most demanding environments for international corporate events, driven by the oil and gas sector’s continued need for bilateral meetings and infrastructure review sessions. The high risk environment (US State Dept Level 4 Do Not Travel, 2024; FCDO advise against all but essential travel, 2024) reflects armed militia activity, kidnapping risk for foreign nationals, and documented attacks on energy sector infrastructure and hotels. Every element of the Basra event security programme – venue selection, access control, delegate transport, and medical planning – is calibrated to this environment. Armed close protection, armoured vehicles, and medical evacuation planning are standard features of the programme, not optional additions.

item.title: Event Security in Bern
item.description: Event security in Bern reflects the city’s dual role as the Swiss federal capital and a hub for intergovernmental and diplomatic conferences alongside its pharmaceutical and financial sector activity. The low risk environment is characterised by specific awareness requirements: demonstration activity near the Bundeshaus, digital espionage risk for conference participants (NDB Annual Report, 2024), and petty crime in tourist zones. All event security personnel are licensed under the Concordat on Private Security (CIPES) at cantonal level, and events involving government principals or armed close protection require coordination with the Kantonspolizei Bern.

item.title: Event Security in Cali
item.description:
Event security in Cali addresses both the operational event requirement – access control, close protection, delegate transport – and the high risk environment that applies to all corporate events in Cali. SuperVigilancia (SVSP - Superintendencia de Vigilancia y Seguridad Privada) means all security personnel must hold current local credentials.

For the broader Cali security picture, see the Cali city briefing and close protection officers in Cali for personal protective cover during the event period. For regional context, see event security in Bogota.

item.description:

item.description:

PSIRA-licensed operators, armed close protection for senior principals, and managed event transport are the foundations of professional event security in eThekwini.

item.title: Event Security in Fortaleza
item.description:

SSPDS-CE (Secretaria da Seguranca Publica e Defesa Social do Ceara) means all security personnel must hold current local credentials.

item.title: Event Security in Guangzhou
item.description:
Guangzhou’s event security environment is defined by the twice-annual Canton Fair cycle, which brings significantly elevated visitor volumes, commercial intelligence activity, and international executive traffic to the city. PSB oversight means all event security is delivered through licensed partner operators, and the national security law framework applies to all international corporate events in mainland China.

For the broader Guangzhou security picture, see the Guangzhou city briefing and secure airport transfers in Guangzhou for airport-to-venue delegate transport.

item.title: Event Security in Havana
item.description: Event security in Havana requires an approach calibrated to Cuba’s distinctive operating environment: no independent private security market, MININT control of all security functions under MININT Order 89/2011, state monitoring of international conferences, and logistics constraints on delegate services. Despite these complexities, Havana hosts an active international event calendar through the FIHAV trade fair, BioCubaFarma biotechnology conferences, and Cuba-Latin America diplomatic and business forums. The moderate risk environment (FCDO Cuba, 2024) includes petty crime in tourist areas, the state monitoring context, and limited emergency response capacity for major incidents.

item.title: Event Security in Ibadan
item.description: Event security in Ibadan operates in a high risk environment (FCDO Nigeria, 2024) driven by armed robbery risk, kidnapping risk for foreign delegates, and carjacking on the Lagos-Ibadan Expressway corridor. Despite these challenges, Ibadan hosts significant international conference activity through the IITA (International Institute of Tropical Agriculture) research hub, University of Ibadan academic gatherings, and Oyo State government bilateral events. Close protection for all foreign delegates, vetted transport for every movement, and medical evacuation planning are standard elements of the event security programme rather than optional additions. All personnel are licensed under the NSCDC Act 2007.

item.title: Event Security in Krakow
item.description: Event security in Krakow covers a diverse event landscape ranging from technology conferences at the ICE Congress Centre and Google Campus events to heritage-venue corporate dinners at the Wieliczka Salt Mine and Wawel Royal Castle. The low risk environment (FCDO Poland, 2024) is characterised by petty crime in tourist districts, vehicle crime at event car parks, and occasional demonstration activity near Old Town venues. All event security personnel are licensed under the Act on Physical Protection of Persons and Property (1997, amended 2022) and hold current credentials from the Komendant Wojewodzki Policji Malopolskiej.

item.title: Event Security in Kumasi
item.description: Event security in Kumasi serves a corporate event landscape driven primarily by mining sector executive meetings, agricultural trade events, and Ashanti regional government bilateral sessions. The moderate risk environment (FCDO Ghana, 2024) is characterised by street crime near market venues, road safety risks on Ghanaian roads (WHO Global Road Safety), and communal tension awareness during chieftaincy or politically sensitive periods. All event security personnel are licensed under the Private Security Organizations Act (ACT 803, 2009) and hold current PSOB registration.

item.title: Event Security in Las Vegas
item.description: Event security in Las Vegas operates in one of the world’s highest-footfall convention environments, with the Las Vegas Convention Center alone hosting events drawing hundreds of thousands of delegates annually. The 1 October 2017 mass shooting fundamentally reshaped Las Vegas’s event security landscape: LVMPD, Clark County, and all major convention venues now operate under revised protocols, and active-threat contingency planning is a standard element of every Las Vegas event security programme. The moderate risk environment requires active management of mass-gathering risk, street crime exposure during peak convention periods, and extreme heat for any outdoor event elements during summer months.

item.description:

All event security personnel deployed in France must hold a current CNAPS Professional Card (Conseil National des Activités Privées de Sécurité, Loi 2012-304), and large events in Marseille are coordinated with the DDSP (Direction Départementale de la Sécurité Publique des Bouches-du-Rhone).

item.description: Event security in Valencia spans a diverse event calendar including major European trade fairs at Feria Valencia, bilateral automotive sector meetings, the Formula One Grand Prix street circuit, and high-level conferences at the Palacio de Congresos. item.description:

item.title: Event Security in Adelaide
item.description:
Adelaide is an active destination for international corporate events and conferences, with Adelaide Convention Centre, Entertainment Centre, Lot Fourteen innovation precinct, and Adelaide Oval providing the principal large-format infrastructure. FCDO advises normal precautions for Australia (2026); FCDO notes extreme summer heat as a health risk in South Australia from December to February. US State Department Level 1 (Exercise Normal Precautions) for Australia as of 2026.

The security profile

SAPOL Crime Statistics 2024 provides the evidential baseline for event security planning in Adelaide: low. SAPOL Crime Statistics 2024 record low overall crime in the CBD and North Adelaide; Hindley Street after 22:00 on weekends is the primary elevated-risk zone. For corporate event planners, the operational risks that matter are transfer security between venues and hotels, access control at large-format or multi-building venues, and close protection for executives and keynote speakers.

item.title: Event Security in Atlanta
item.description: Atlanta is a major corporate event destination, hosting events at State Farm Arena, Mercedes-Benz Stadium, and the Georgia World Congress Center. Event security in Atlanta is regulated under OCGA Title 43, Ch 38, requiring all deployed personnel to hold current licences from the Georgia Secretary of State. The threat context from the APD Crime Report 2025 (carjacking citywide (APD Crime Report 2025)) informs both venue selection guidance and the emergency response protocols built into every event security plan.

item.title: Event Security in Austin
item.description:
Austin is an active destination for international corporate events and conferences, with Austin Convention Center, The Domain tech campuses, and UT Austin research facilities providing the principal large-format infrastructure. FCDO advises normal precautions for the United States (2026); no specific advisory for Austin. US State Department Level 1 (Exercise Normal Precautions) for the United States as of 2026.

The security profile

APD Annual Report 2024 provides the evidential baseline for event security planning in Austin: moderate. APD Annual Report 2024 records elevated property crime and vehicle theft, concentrated in outer East Austin and some Downtown parking structures. For corporate event planners, the operational risks that matter are transfer security between venues and hotels, access control at large-format or multi-building venues, and close protection for executives and keynote speakers.

item.title: Event Security in Barcelona
item.description: Barcelona event security operates under Spain’s Law 5/2014 on Private Security (Ley de Seguridad Privada), overseen by the Interior Ministry (Ministerio del Interior), covering principal venues including the CCIB at Diagonal Mar, Palau de Congressos de Catalunya, and Fira Barcelona Gran Via. Core service components span multi-tier access control, delegate close protection, and transfer management from Barcelona El Prat Airport (BCN). The city hosts Mobile World Congress annually (100,000-plus delegates at Fira Gran Via), and the documented concentration of street crime in tourist areas, combined with the historical context of the 2017 Las Ramblas attack, means delegate movement planning extends beyond venue perimeters to street-level escort protocols.

item.title: Event Security in Boston
item.description: Boston is a major corporate event destination, hosting events at TD Garden, Boston Convention and Exhibition Center, and Harvard-adjacent venues. Event security in Boston is regulated under MGL c.147, ss.22-30, requiring all deployed personnel to hold current licences from the Massachusetts Department of Public Safety. The threat context from the BPD Crime Report 2025 (very low) informs both venue selection guidance and the emergency response protocols built into every event security plan.

item.title: Event Security in Brussels
item.description: Brussels event security operates under Belgium’s Law of 2 October 2017 on private and special security, overseen by the Interior Ministry (SPF Interieur / FOD Binnenlandse Zaken), and applies across the city’s principal venues: Square Brussels Convention Centre, Brussels Expo, and the European Parliament International Conference Centre. Core service components cover multi-tier access control, delegate close protection, and transfer management from Brussels Airport (BRU). The city’s dual role as the EU political capital and a NATO partner seat means many events carry a diplomatic or high-VIP profile that requires coordination with Belgian Federal Police and, where applicable, institutional security services.

item.title: Event Security in Chennai
item.description:
Chennai is an active destination for international corporate events and conferences, with Chennai Trade Centre, ITC Grand Chola, Taj Coromandel, and Hyatt Regency Chennai providing the principal large-format infrastructure. FCDO advises normal precautions for India (2026); FCDO advises specific awareness of monsoon season disruption in Tamil Nadu from October to December. US State Department Level 2 (Exercise Increased Caution) for India as of 2026.

The security profile

Tamil Nadu Police Crime Statistics 2024 provides the evidential baseline for event security planning in Chennai: moderate. Tamil Nadu Police Crime Statistics 2024 record elevated road traffic incidents and petty theft in crowded market areas; monsoon season from October to December introduces additional movement disruption. For corporate event planners, the operational risks that matter are transfer security between venues and hotels, access control at large-format or multi-building venues, and close protection for executives and keynote speakers.

item.title: Event Security in Chicago
item.description: Chicago is a major corporate event destination, hosting events at McCormick Place Convention Center, United Center, and Soldier Field. Event security in Chicago is regulated under Illinois PSISA 2004 (IDFPR), requiring all deployed personnel to hold current licences from the Illinois Department of Financial and Professional Regulation. The threat context from the CPD Crime Report 2025 (carjacking elevated citywide (CPD Crime Report 2025)) informs both venue selection guidance and the emergency response protocols built into every event security plan.

item.title: Event Security in Edinburgh
item.description: Edinburgh event security operates under the UK’s Private Security Industry Act 2001, with all operators licensed by the Security Industry Authority (SIA), covering principal venues including the Edinburgh International Conference Centre (EICC), Assembly Rooms Edinburgh, and Murrayfield Stadium. Core service components span SIA-licensed access control, delegate close protection, and transfer management from Edinburgh Airport (EDI). Edinburgh’s August Festival and Fringe, which brings approximately one million visitors to the city, creates Europe’s largest concentrated public event environment outside major sporting competitions, requiring event security planning that accounts for elevated street-level crowd density across the Old Town and Royal Mile in addition to standard venue security protocols.

item.title: Event Security in Frankfurt
item.description: Frankfurt event security operates under Germany’s Trade Regulation Act (Gewerbeordnung) Section 34a, with Hessian oversight from the Hessisches Landeskriminalamt (HLKA), covering principal venues including Messe Frankfurt, Kap Europa Congress Center Frankfurt, and Frankfurt Congress Center. Core service components span credentialled access control, delegate close protection, and transfer management from Frankfurt Airport (FRA) Fraport. Frankfurt’s role as the European Central Bank seat and Germany’s primary financial hub means many corporate events carry a financial sector or regulatory profile requiring coordination with Bankenviertel security protocols alongside standard Messe venue procedures.

item.title: Event Security in Hamburg
item.description: Hamburg event security operates under Germany’s Trade Regulation Act (Gewerbeordnung) Section 34a, with Hamburg-state oversight from the Landeskriminalamt Hamburg (LKA Hamburg), covering principal venues including the CCH Congress Center Hamburg, Hamburg Messe, and Elbphilharmonie. Core service components span credentialled access control, delegate close protection, and transfer management from Hamburg Airport (HAM) Helmut Schmidt. Hamburg’s position as Germany’s largest port city and a major media and shipping sector hub means many events carry a mixed delegate profile, and the G20 Hamburg Summit (2017) established public-order planning frameworks that continue to inform security coordination with Hamburg Police for large international gatherings.

item.title: Event Security in Hanoi
item.description:
Hanoi is an active destination for international corporate events and conferences, with National Convention Centre, Lotte Center Hanoi, Melia Hanoi, and the Ba Dinh embassy district providing the principal large-format infrastructure. FCDO advises normal precautions for Vietnam (2026); FCDO notes road traffic conditions as a specific risk, particularly motorbike density. US State Department Level 1 (Exercise Normal Precautions) for Vietnam as of 2026.

The security profile

Ministry of Public Security Vietnam Crime Statistics 2024 provides the evidential baseline for event security planning in Hanoi: moderate. Ministry of Public Security Vietnam 2024 records elevated motorbike bag-snatching in the Old Quarter and tourist-dense areas; road traffic incidents involving foreigners are a consistent risk. For corporate event planners, the operational risks that matter are transfer security between venues and hotels, access control at large-format or multi-building venues, and close protection for executives and keynote speakers.

item.description: Houston is a major corporate event destination, hosting events at George R. Brown Convention Center, Minute Maid Park, and Toyota Center. The threat context from the HPD Crime Report 2025 (property crime and vehicle theft elevated (HPD Crime Report 2025)) informs both venue selection guidance and the emergency response protocols built into every event security plan.

item.title: Event Security in Hyderabad
item.description:

The security profile

item.title: Event Security in Lisbon
item.description: Lisbon’s event security framework operates under Portugal’s Law 34/2013 (Lei de Seguranca Privada), overseen by the Policia de Seguranca Publica (PSP), covering all licensed operators at venues including the Centro de Congressos de Lisboa, Altice Arena, and Pavilhao de Portugal. Core service components span access control and credentialling, delegate close protection, and airport transfer management from Lisbon Humberto Delgado Airport (LIS). The city’s role as an annual host to the Web Summit and its growth as a European tech hub have raised demand for structured delegate security planning.

item.title: Event Security in Los Angeles
item.description: Los Angeles is a major corporate event destination, hosting events at Staples Center/Crypto.com Arena, the LA Convention Center, and private studio event spaces. item.title: Event Security in Luxembourg City
item.description: Luxembourg City event security operates under the Law of 12 November 2002 on private security, overseen by the Grand Ducal Police (Police Grand-Ducale), covering principal venues including the European Convention Centre Luxembourg (ECCL) at Kirchberg Plateau, LuxExpo The Box, and Chambre de Commerce Luxembourg. Core service components span credentialled access control, delegate close protection, and transfer management from Luxembourg Airport Findel (LUX). The Kirchberg Plateau’s concentration of EU institutions, including the European Court of Justice, Court of Auditors, and European Investment Bank, means many events carry a diplomatic or institutional profile requiring coordination with EU institutional security services alongside standard licensed private security.

item.title: Event Security in Lyon
item.description: Lyon event security operates under France’s Law 2011-267 (Loppsi 2) and Book VI of the Internal Security Code, with CNAPS (Conseil National des Activites Privees de Securite) as the licensing authority, covering principal venues including the Centre de Congres de Lyon, Cite Internationale de Lyon, and Eurexpo Lyon. Core service components span CNAPS-regulated access control, delegate close protection, and transfer management from Lyon-Saint Exupery Airport (LYS) and the adjacent TGV station. France’s Vigipirate system is at Emergency Attack Enhanced level for major events as of 2026, which means event security planning in Lyon incorporates heightened perimeter protocols and Police Nationale coordination as a baseline requirement rather than an optional layer.

item.description: Manchester is a major corporate event destination, hosting events at Manchester Arena, Manchester Central Convention Complex, and Old Trafford for large-scale corporate and entertainment events. item.description: Reykjavik event security operates under Iceland’s Act no. 35/2008 on Security Services, overseen by the National Police Commissioner (Rikislögreglustjórinn), covering the city’s principal international venue, Harpa Reykjavik Concert Hall and Conference Centre. Core service components span credentialled access control, delegate close protection, and transfer management from Keflavik International Airport (KEF), which sits 50km from the city centre. Iceland’s status as the top-ranked country in the Institute for Economics and Peace Global Peace Index 2025 means the primary operational complexity in Reykjavik is logistical, namely the KEF transfer distance and Harpa’s position as the single venue for all major international gatherings, rather than threat-response.

item.title: Event Security in San Francisco
item.description: San Francisco is a major corporate event destination, hosting events at Moscone Convention Center, Chase Center, and the Ferry Building for corporate functions. Event security in San Francisco is regulated under California BSIS, Business and Professions Code, requiring all deployed personnel to hold current licences from the California BSIS. The threat context from the SFPD Crime Report 2025 (vehicle break-ins among highest in California (SFPD Crime Report 2025)) informs both venue selection guidance and the emergency response protocols built into every event security plan.

item.title: Event Security in Seoul
item.description:
Seoul is an active destination for international corporate events and conferences, with COEX convention centre, Samsung and Hyundai headquarters, Jongno embassy district providing the principal large-format infrastructure. FCDO advises normal precautions for South Korea (2026); FCDO notes North Korea geopolitical context. US State Department Level 1 (Exercise Normal Precautions) for South Korea as of 2026.

The security profile

NPA Crime Statistics 2024 provides the evidential baseline for event security planning in Seoul: low. NPA Crime Statistics 2024 record low violent crime citywide, with well-policed corporate zones in Gangnam, Yeouido, and Jongno. For corporate event planners, the operational risks that matter are transfer security between venues and hotels, access control at large-format or multi-building venues, and close protection for executives and keynote speakers.

item.title: Event Security in Taipei
item.description: Taipei is a major corporate event destination, hosting events at Taipei International Convention Center (TICC), Taipei Arena, and the Nangang Exhibition Center. Event security in Taipei is regulated under Private Security Services Industry Act 2009, NPA/MOI, requiring all deployed personnel to hold current licences from the National Police Agency (NPA), Ministry of the Interior. The threat context from the Taiwan NPA Crime Statistics 2024 (very low crime (Taiwan NPA Crime Statistics 2024)) informs both venue selection guidance and the emergency response protocols built into every event security plan.

item.title: Event Security in Vancouver
item.description: Vancouver is a major corporate event destination, hosting events at Vancouver Convention Centre, Rogers Arena, and BC Place for large-scale corporate and entertainment events. Event security in Vancouver is regulated under BC Security Services Act 2007, requiring all deployed personnel to hold current licences from the BC Ministry of Public Safety, Security Programs Division. The threat context from the VPD Statistical Report 2025 (Downtown Eastside opioid crisis elevated property crime (VPD Statistical Report 2025)) informs both venue selection guidance and the emergency response protocols built into every event security plan.

item.title: Event Security in Wellington
item.description:
Wellington is an active destination for international corporate events and conferences, with Michael Fowler Centre, Te Papa Museum events, Westpac Stadium, and the Beehive government precinct providing the principal large-format infrastructure. FCDO advises normal precautions for New Zealand (2026); FCDO notes seismic risk and advises checking NZ government emergency management guidance. US State Department Level 1 (Exercise Normal Precautions) for New Zealand as of 2026.

The security profile

New Zealand Police Crime Statistics 2024 provides the evidential baseline for event security planning in Wellington: low. New Zealand Police Crime Statistics 2024 record low overall crime; opportunistic vehicle break-ins and property theft are the primary concern in the CBD. For corporate event planners, the operational risks that matter are transfer security between venues and hotels, access control at large-format or multi-building venues, and close protection for executives and keynote speakers.

item.title: Event Security in Athens
item.description:
Event security in Athens is governed by Greece’s Presidential Decree 78/2011 on Security Guards Licensing framework. Athens hosts corporate, financial, and sector-specific conferences at Metropolitan Expo Athens (Spata, adjacent to ATH) and Hotel Grande Bretagne Athens infrastructure. Pre-event assessment, delegate close protection, and vetted airport transfers from ATH are the core components of the Athens event security programme.

Greece regulatory framework

All event security personnel at Athens events hold current Greece credentials under Presidential Decree 78/2011 on Security Guards Licensing. Hellenic Police (Elliniki Astynomia) and Ministry of Citizen Protection (YPDM) provides oversight.

item.title: Event Security in Belgrade
item.description:
Event security in Belgrade is governed by Serbia’s Law on Private Security Activity (Official Gazette of RS No. 104/2013) framework. Belgrade hosts corporate, financial, and sector-specific conferences at Belgrade Fair (Beogradski Sajam) and Crowne Plaza Belgrade infrastructure. Pre-event assessment, delegate close protection, and vetted airport transfers from BEG are the core components of the Belgrade event security programme.

Serbia regulatory framework

All event security personnel at Belgrade events hold current Serbia credentials under Law on Private Security Activity (Official Gazette of RS No. 104/2013). Serbian Ministry of Interior (Ministarstvo unutrasnjih poslova, MUP) provides oversight.

item.title: Event Security in Bucharest
item.description:
Event security in Bucharest is governed by Romania’s Law 333/2003 on the Guard of Objectives, Goods, Values and Personal Protection framework. Bucharest hosts corporate, financial, and sector-specific conferences at Romexpo Exhibition Centre and JW Marriott Bucharest Grand Hotel infrastructure. Pre-event assessment, delegate close protection, and vetted airport transfers from OTP are the core components of the Bucharest event security programme.

Romania regulatory framework

All event security personnel at Bucharest events hold current Romania credentials under Law 333/2003 on the Guard of Objectives, Goods, Values and Personal Protection. Romanian Ministry of Internal Affairs (Ministerul Afacerilor Interne) provides oversight.

item.title: Event Security in Budapest
item.description:
Event security in Budapest is governed by Hungary’s Act CXXXIII of 2005 on Security Services and on the Activities of Private Investigators framework. Budapest hosts corporate, financial, and sector-specific conferences at Hungexpo Budapest and Four Seasons Hotel Gresham Palace Budapest infrastructure. Pre-event assessment, delegate close protection, and vetted airport transfers from BUD are the core components of the Budapest event security programme.

Hungary regulatory framework

All event security personnel at Budapest events hold current Hungary credentials under Act CXXXIII of 2005 on Security Services and on the Activities of Private Investigators. Hungarian National Police (Rendorseg) provides oversight.

item.title: Event Security in Copenhagen
item.description:
Event security in Copenhagen is governed by Denmark’s Security Guard Act (Vagtvirksomhedsloven) framework. Copenhagen hosts corporate, financial, and sector-specific conferences at Bella Center (Copenhagen Exhibition and Congress Centre) and Hotel d’Angleterre Copenhagen infrastructure. Pre-event assessment, delegate close protection, and vetted airport transfers from CPH are the core components of the Copenhagen event security programme.

Denmark regulatory framework

All event security personnel at Copenhagen events hold current Denmark credentials under Security Guard Act (Vagtvirksomhedsloven). Danish Police (Rigspolitiet) and Danish Security and Intelligence Service (PET) provides oversight.

item.title: Event Security in Dublin
item.description:
Event security in Dublin is governed by Ireland’s Private Security Services Act 2004 (as amended) framework. Dublin hosts corporate, financial, and sector-specific conferences at Convention Centre Dublin (CCD) and Intercontinental Dublin (Ballsbridge) infrastructure. Pre-event assessment, delegate close protection, and vetted airport transfers from DUB are the core components of the Dublin event security programme.

Ireland regulatory framework

All event security personnel at Dublin events hold current Ireland credentials under Private Security Services Act 2004 (as amended). Private Security Authority (PSA) provides oversight.

item.title: Event Security in Helsinki
item.description:
Event security in Helsinki is governed by Finland’s Finnish Private Security Services Act (1085/2015) framework. Helsinki hosts corporate, financial, and sector-specific conferences at Messukeskus Helsinki (Expo and Convention Centre) and Hotel Kamp Helsinki infrastructure. Pre-event assessment, delegate close protection, and vetted airport transfers from HEL are the core components of the Helsinki event security programme.

Finland regulatory framework

All event security personnel at Helsinki events hold current Finland credentials under Finnish Private Security Services Act (1085/2015). National Police Board (Poliisihallitus) provides oversight.

item.title: Event Security in Oslo
item.description:
Event security in Oslo is governed by Norway’s Security Services Act (Vaktvirksomhetsloven, 2018) framework. Oslo hosts corporate, financial, and sector-specific conferences at Oslo Spektrum and The Thief Oslo infrastructure. Pre-event assessment, delegate close protection, and vetted airport transfers from OSL are the core components of the Oslo event security programme.

Norway regulatory framework

All event security personnel at Oslo events hold current Norway credentials under Security Services Act (Vaktvirksomhetsloven, 2018). Norwegian Security Association (NSR) and Norwegian Police Security Service (PST) provides oversight.

item.title: Event Security in Prague
item.description:
Event security in Prague is governed by Czech Republic’s Act No. 69/2006 Coll. on Security Activities framework. Prague hosts corporate, financial, and sector-specific conferences at Prague Congress Centre (PCC) and Mandarin Oriental Prague infrastructure. Pre-event assessment, delegate close protection, and vetted airport transfers from PRG are the core components of the Prague event security programme.

Czech Republic regulatory framework

All event security personnel at Prague events hold current Czech Republic credentials under Act No. 69/2006 Coll. on Security Activities. Czech Ministry of the Interior (Ministerstvo vnitra) provides oversight.

item.title: Event Security in Stockholm
item.description:
Event security in Stockholm is governed by Sweden’s Security Companies Act (Lag 1974:191 om bevakningsforetag) framework. Stockholm hosts corporate, financial, and sector-specific conferences at Stockholmsmassan (Stockholm International Fairs, Alvsjo) and Grand Hotel Stockholm infrastructure. Pre-event assessment, delegate close protection, and vetted airport transfers from ARN are the core components of the Stockholm event security programme.

Sweden regulatory framework

All event security personnel at Stockholm events hold current Sweden credentials under Security Companies Act (Lag 1974:191 om bevakningsforetag). Swedish Police Authority (Polismyndigheten) provides oversight.

item.title: Event Security in Bratislava, Slovakia
item.description:
Event security in Bratislava addresses the specific operational requirements of internationally attended conferences and summits in Slovakia’s low risk environment. Normal precautions for slovakia (2026); US State Dept Level 1 (Exercise Normal Precautions) for Slovakia (2026).

Effective event security planning for Bratislava begins with an intelligence review covering the current local political situation, known protest scheduling, and active Slovakia advisories. Venue access management at Kempinski River Park Bratislava (Dvorakovo nabrezie 6) and other principal conference properties requires credential verification, zone segregation, and specific arrival protocols for senior delegates. Secure transport from Milan Rastislav Stefanik Airport (BTS) and medevac pre-arrangement to Vienna or Prague are standard components of the pre-event plan.

item.title: Event Security in Chisinau, Moldova
item.description: Chisinau’s growing importance as a centre for EU accession events, energy transition conferences and governance reform forums reflects Moldova’s rapid geopolitical reorientation since 2022. The FCDO Moldova travel advice (2026) advises a high degree of caution, and the Transnistrian frozen conflict combined with Moldova’s proximity to the Ukraine war theatre means that event security planning must incorporate contingency protocols for rapid escalation, in addition to standard access control, secure transport and credential management. For the full Chisinau security context and city-level threat assessment, see our Chisinau city security overview.

item.title: Event Security in Cotonou, Benin
item.description: Cotonou’s role as Benin’s commercial engine and principal port city makes it an active destination for trade, agribusiness and development sector events, attracting international delegates from Europe, North America and across West Africa throughout the year. The FCDO Benin travel advice (2026) advises a high degree of caution, and while Cotonou is not directly affected by the jihadist activity that has destabilised Benin’s northern departments, the medium-risk environment requires professional event security with structured access management, secure transport and a pre-arranged medical evacuation protocol. For the broader Cotonou security context and city-level threat assessment, see our Cotonou city security overview.

item.title: Event Security in Ljubljana, Slovenia
item.description:
Event security in Ljubljana addresses the specific operational requirements of internationally attended conferences and summits in Slovenia’s low risk environment. Normal precautions for slovenia (2026); US State Dept Level 1 (Exercise Normal Precautions) for Slovenia (2026).

Effective event security planning for Ljubljana begins with an intelligence review covering the current local political situation, known protest scheduling, and active Slovenia advisories. Venue access management at InterContinental Ljubljana (Slovenska cesta 59) and other principal conference properties requires credential verification, zone segregation, and specific arrival protocols for senior delegates. Secure transport from Ljubljana Joze Pucnik Airport (LJU) and medevac pre-arrangement to Vienna or Milan are standard components of the pre-event plan.

item.title: Event Security in Lome, Togo
item.description: Lome’s emergence as a West African logistics and trade hub, anchored by the deepwater Port of Lome, makes it an increasingly active destination for investment conferences, maritime trade forums and ECOWAS-linked commercial meetings. While Togo’s medium-risk environment is less acute than some regional capitals, the FCDO Togo travel advice (2026) advises a high degree of caution and the Gnassingbe government’s political management of public assembly means that event security planning must incorporate demonstration monitoring, secure transport and vetted venue access management. For the broader Lome security context and threat profile, see our Lome city security overview.

item.title: Event Security in Malabo, Equatorial Guinea
item.description: Malabo’s position as the capital of one of sub-Saharan Africa’s most significant oil-producing states draws international energy executives, investors and diplomatic representatives to Bioko Island throughout the year, but Equatorial Guinea’s authoritarian governance, very limited medical infrastructure and Gulf of Guinea maritime environment require that professional event security is treated as an operational priority from the earliest planning stages. The FCDO Equatorial Guinea travel advice (2026) advises a high degree of caution, and the US State Department Level 2 advisory (2026) cites corruption and limited rule of law as ongoing concerns for international visitors. For the broader Malabo security context, including threat assessments relevant to individual delegates, see our Malabo city security overview.

item.title: Event Security in Minsk, Belarus
item.description:
Event security in Minsk addresses the specific operational requirements of internationally attended conferences and summits in Belarus’s high risk environment. Advises against all but essential travel to belarus (2026); arbitrary detention risk is explicitly cited; US State Dept Level 4 (Do Not Travel) for Belarus (2026).

Effective event security planning for Minsk begins with an intelligence review covering the current local political situation, known protest scheduling, and active Belarus advisories. Venue access management at Minsk Marriott Hotel (Kirova 13) and other principal conference properties requires credential verification, zone segregation, and specific arrival protocols for senior delegates. Secure transport from Minsk National Airport (MSQ) and medevac pre-arrangement to Vilnius (Lithuania) or Warsaw (Poland) are standard components of the pre-event plan.

item.title: Event Security in Nicosia, Cyprus
item.description:
Event security in Nicosia addresses the specific operational requirements of internationally attended conferences and summits in Cyprus’s low risk environment. Normal precautions for the republic of cyprus (2026); fcdo advises against all travel to the turkish-controlled north; US State Dept Level 1 (Exercise Normal Precautions) for Cyprus (2026).

Effective event security planning for Nicosia begins with an intelligence review covering the current local political situation, known protest scheduling, and active Cyprus advisories. Venue access management at Hilton Nicosia (Archbishop Makarios III Avenue) and other principal conference properties requires credential verification, zone segregation, and specific arrival protocols for senior delegates. Secure transport from Larnaca International Airport (LCA) and medevac pre-arrangement to Athens or Tel Aviv are standard components of the pre-event plan.

item.title: Event Security in Podgorica, Montenegro
item.description: Podgorica’s role as the capital of a NATO member and advanced EU accession candidate makes it a regular venue for diplomatically significant international conferences and investment forums, but the city’s specific organised crime environment, particularly the Kavac and Skaljari clan conflict highlighted by the FCDO Montenegro travel advice (2026), means that event security planning must address bystander risk and strict movement management as core operational priorities, not merely background considerations. For the full Podgorica security context and city-level risk assessment, see our Podgorica city security overview.

item.title: Event Security in Praia, Cape Verde
item.description: Praia offers one of the most stable and low-risk event-security environments in the broader West Africa and Atlantic region, backed by Cape Verde’s consistent democratic governance and the FCDO’s standard-precautions advice (2026) and US State Department Level 1 designation. The city’s growing MICE profile, driven by Blue Economy summits, renewable energy forums and ECOWAS-linked commercial meetings, means that internationally attended conferences are becoming a regular feature of the Cape Verdean capital’s calendar. For the full Praia city security context and background on the country’s governance environment, see our Praia city security overview.

item.title: Event Security in Riga, Latvia
item.description:
Event security in Riga addresses the specific operational requirements of internationally attended conferences and summits in Latvia’s low risk environment. Normal precautions for latvia (2026); US State Dept Level 1 (Exercise Normal Precautions) for Latvia (2026).

Effective event security planning for Riga begins with an intelligence review covering the current local political situation, known protest scheduling, and active Latvia advisories. Venue access management at Radisson Blu Latvija Conference and Spa Hotel (Elizabetes 55) and other principal conference properties requires credential verification, zone segregation, and specific arrival protocols for senior delegates. Secure transport from Riga International Airport (RIX) and medevac pre-arrangement to Stockholm or Helsinki are standard components of the pre-event plan.

item.title: Event Security in Sarajevo, Bosnia and Herzegovina
