Event security in the city requires specific attention to the state surveillance environment, thorough attendee accreditation, and medical evacuation planning that reflects Tashkent’s limited local medical capacity.

For the broader Tashkent security context, see our Tashkent city page for the current SNB surveillance assessment and regulatory framework. For delegates requiring personal close protection throughout the Tashkent event programme, bodyguard hire in Tashkent covers close protection deployment in Uzbekistan.

item.title: Event Security in Tbilisi
item.description:
Tbilisi event security addresses a city in transition: a growing South Caucasus conference hub with an active intelligence environment driven by its post-2022 role as a relocation centre for Russia-linked business, and a domestic political landscape that places civil unrest on the event contingency checklist in a way that most comparable European small capitals do not require.

For the broader Tbilisi security context, see our Tbilisi city page for the Ministry of Internal Affairs licensing framework and the regional instability assessment. For delegates requiring personal close protection at Tbilisi events, bodyguard hire in Tbilisi covers close protection deployment in Georgia.

item.title: Event Security in Warsaw
item.description: Warsaw is Central Europe’s rising conference hub and a key location for NATO-adjacent defence events, EU policy summits, and Ukraine reconstruction planning. Poland’s ABW documents active Russian and Belarusian intelligence operations targeting Warsaw in these event categories. Polish licensing under the Act on Physical Protection of Persons and Property governs all deployed personnel. Counter-intelligence briefing and ABW-aware event security planning are standard elements for defence and Ukraine-sector events. For the broader Warsaw security picture see our Warsaw city page and bodyguard hire in Warsaw.

item.title: Event Security in Abidjan
item.description: Abidjan is West Africa’s principal commercial city and a recurring host for ECOWAS summits and large international conferences at Hotel Ivoire and Sofitel Abidjan. The FCDO advises heightened awareness of terrorism risk in Ivory Coast, linked to instability in the Sahel region to the north, and this assessment shapes every event security plan we develop for the city. Private security operators in Abidjan are licensed by the Ministere de l’Interieur, and we engage only verified, licensed local partners for armed response and close protection deployments. For delegates planning to attend conferences in Abidjan, our Abidjan city security overview sets out the broader threat environment and transport considerations.

item.title: Event Security in Abuja
item.description: Abuja serves as Nigeria’s federal capital and a recurring host city for ECOWAS, African Union and government-adjacent conferences, most commonly at the Transcorp Hilton Abuja, which is the FCT’s primary international conference property. The FCDO assesses Nigeria as a high-risk destination, citing terrorism, kidnapping and civil unrest as active threats that apply across the country, including in the FCT. Private security companies operating in Nigeria must be licensed by the Nigeria Security and Civil Defence Corps (NSCDC) under the NSCDC Act 2003, and we engage only registered, licensed local partners for all armed response and guarding roles. For delegates and event organisers, our Abuja city security overview provides a fuller picture of the FCT operating environment.

item.title: Event Security in Accra
item.description: Accra has emerged as a preferred host city for international conferences and ECOWAS-related meetings, partly because Ghana’s comparatively stable political environment offers a more predictable operating context than many regional alternatives. The Accra International Conference Centre, Kempinski Hotel Gold Coast City and Labadi Beach Hotel are the primary properties for summit-level events. Ghana’s Private Security Organisations Act 2020 establishes the licensing framework for private security companies operating in the country, and we engage only Act-compliant operators for guarding, crowd management and close protection roles in Accra. For a fuller picture of the security environment, see our Accra city security overview.

item.title: Event Security in Addis Ababa
item.description: Addis Ababa is Africa’s diplomatic capital, hosting the headquarters of the African Union and serving as a regular venue for AU summits, IGAD meetings and World Health Assembly for Africa sessions at the UN Conference Centre, Sheraton Addis and Hilton Addis. Ethiopia’s Ministry of Trade and Industry licences private security companies operating in the country, and we engage only registered, licensed local partners for all operational roles. The FCDO rates Ethiopia as a high-risk destination due to regional conflict and instability, and this assessment directly shapes the planning framework we apply to every event brief in the city. For the broader operating environment, our Addis Ababa city security overview provides relevant context on transport, threat categories and emergency contacts.

item.title: Event Security in Algiers
item.description: Algiers is a working venue for government-adjacent trade events and international business meetings, most notably the Foire Internationale d’Alger at the Palais des Expositions, which draws exhibitors and delegations from across North Africa and the wider Mediterranean region. Algeria’s Ministry of Interior licences private security companies operating in the country, and we engage only registered, licensed local operators for all guarding and close protection roles. The FCDO advises a high degree of vigilance in Algeria, citing residual terrorism risk, and this advisory shapes the threat-aware planning framework we apply to every Algiers event brief. For a fuller picture of the operating environment, see our Algiers city security overview.

item.title: Event Security in Almaty
item.description: Almaty is Kazakhstan’s largest city and its primary commercial and conference centre, hosting SCO-related meetings, international business events and diplomatic gatherings at venues including Almaty Arena, the Almaty International Conference Hall and Rahat Palace Hotel. Kazakhstan’s Ministry of Internal Affairs (MIA) licences all private security operators, and we engage only MIA-compliant partners for every role in our Almaty deployments. The January 2022 civil unrest, which saw significant incidents in Almaty specifically, is a concrete reference point for crowd management and emergency planning: every event security brief we develop for the city incorporates enhanced protocols that reflect that experience. Our Almaty city security overview provides broader context on the security environment and transport infrastructure.

item.title: Event Security in Amman
item.description: Amman’s reputation as the Middle East’s most accessible conference city rests on Jordan’s comparatively stable political environment, its central regional location and an established international hotel and venues infrastructure. The InterContinental Amman and Four Seasons Amman are the primary properties for summit-level events, while Zara Expo handles larger exhibition formats. Jordan’s Public Security Directorate licences private security operators, and we engage only registered, licensed local partners for all roles. The FCDO advises a degree of vigilance in Jordan, noting terrorism risk heightened by proximity to regional conflicts in Syria and Iraq. This advisory is built into every event security plan we develop for Amman. For a fuller picture of the security environment, see our Amman city security overview.

item.title: Event Security in Ankara
item.description: Ankara is Turkey’s capital and the seat of the Grand National Assembly, making it a recurring host for NATO-related diplomatic meetings, intergovernmental conferences and government-adjacent business events at venues including Congresium Ankara and Bilkent Otel. Turkey’s private security sector is governed by Law No. 5188 (Ozel Guvenlik Hizmetlerine Dair Kanun), and all operators and individual personnel must be licensed under this framework. We engage only Law 5188-licensed local partners for every role in Ankara deployments. The FCDO advises heightened vigilance throughout Turkey, with specific reference to PKK-related terrorism and the risk of symbolic attacks in the capital. Counter-terrorism emergency planning is a non-negotiable element of every event brief we accept in Ankara. For broader context on the operating environment, see our Ankara city security overview.

item.title: Event Security in Auckland
item.description:
Auckland event security serves the corporate conference, private function, and HNWI event market in New Zealand’s lowest-risk major urban environment. New Zealand Private Security Personnel and Private Investigators Act 2010 CoA licensing provides the regulatory framework; SkyCity Convention Centre co-ordination and Waiheke Island maritime logistics are the operationally distinctive Auckland event security features.

New Zealand CoA regulatory compliance

Auckland event security companies hold business CoA licences; individual personnel hold crowd controller, property guard, or PPO CoA classes as appropriate. New Zealand Police Licensing and Vetting Service register confirms all status.

item.title: Event Security in Buenos Aires
item.description:
Buenos Aires event security addresses the express kidnapping threat specific to Argentina’s security environment alongside the high frequency of civil unrest that affects access routes and event vicinity risk. Ley 12.297 and CABA municipal licensing provide the regulatory framework; departure transport management is the most operationally distinctive Buenos Aires event security component.

Argentine regulatory compliance

Event security companies hold provincial Ley 12.297 or CABA municipal registration; individual guards hold credenciales habilitantes. Company registration is confirmed for the venue’s specific jurisdiction before deployment.

item.title: Event Security in Cairo
item.description:
Cairo event security operates in a context of an FCDO-assessed likely terrorism threat, extensive state surveillance, and road safety conditions that require substantive vehicle departure management at event endings. Ministry of Interior licensing through the Central Administration for Private Security provides the regulatory framework.

Egyptian Ministry of Interior regulatory compliance

Cairo event security companies hold Ministry of Interior operating permits; individual personnel hold Ministry of Interior authorisations. Company-level licensing is confirmed before deployment, with government-adjacent events requiring established Ministry of Interior co-ordination relationships.

item.title: Event Security in Jakarta
item.description:
Jakarta event security addresses an FCDO very likely terrorism threat with a documented attack history at international business hotels, extreme traffic congestion that requires active departure management, and civil unrest monitoring for demonstration activity near the Sudirman-Senayan event corridor. POLRI licensing under PP 60/2017 and Polda Metro Jaya notification requirements define the regulatory framework.

Indonesian POLRI regulatory compliance

Jakarta event security companies hold POLRI operating licences; individual security personnel hold KTA SATPAM credentials. Event notification (izin keramaian) to Polda Metro Jaya is confirmed for notifiable events.

item.title: Event Security in Manila
item.description:
Manila event security addresses a critical OSAC-rated KFR environment where event departure management, access control against KFR surveillance, and armed force posture determination are standard planning considerations specific to the Philippine security context. PNP and SOSIA licensing under RA 5487 provides the regulatory framework.

Philippine regulatory compliance

Manila event security companies hold current SOSIA company licences; individual personnel hold PNP Security Guard Licences. Armed personnel hold PNP firearm authorisations. PNP district command notification is confirmed for events above the statutory threshold.

item.title: Event Security in Melbourne
item.description: Melbourne hosts some of the Southern Hemisphere’s most prominent sporting and cultural events, drawing large international audiences to venues including the MCG, Rod Laver Arena and the Melbourne Convention and Exhibition Centre. Victoria’s Security Industry Act 1997 sets a clear licensing framework for all private security operatives, with Consumer Affairs Victoria as the licensing authority. Every crowd controller and security guard we deploy in Melbourne holds a current Consumer Affairs Victoria licence, and our event security plans are developed in coordination with Victoria Police event planning teams. For a fuller picture of Melbourne’s security environment, see our Melbourne city security overview.

item.title: Event Security in Miami
item.description:
Miami event security serves the HNWI, corporate, and entertainment sector event calendar concentrated in Art Basel, the Miami Grand Prix, and the South Florida winter season. Florida PSA Chapter 493 licensing through the Division of Licensing provides the regulatory standard; HNWI targeting at event departures and yacht-integrated maritime security are the Miami-specific event security considerations.

Florida Chapter 493 regulatory compliance

Miami event security companies hold Class D Security Agency licences; individual personnel hold Class D (and Class G if armed) licences from the Florida Division of Licensing. All licence statuses are confirmed before deployment.

item.title: Event Security in Milan
item.description: Milan is one of Europe’s principal cities for trade exhibitions, luxury events and international conferences, drawing hundreds of thousands of delegates annually to venues such as Fiera Milano and MiCo - Milan Convention Centre. Italy’s private security sector is tightly regulated under the Testo Unico delle Leggi di Pubblica Sicurezza (TULPS), which requires security companies and individual operatives to hold Prefettura-issued licences before operating in the region. Our partner network in Milan comprises only TULPS-compliant Istituti di Vigilanza Privata, and all event security plans are submitted to the Questura in accordance with Italian public event regulations. For a broader picture of the operating environment, see our Milan city security overview.

item.title: Event Security in Tel Aviv
item.description:
Tel Aviv event security operates in an active conflict environment – FCDO advises against all travel to Israel – where rocket alert shelter protocol integration, armed event security, and vehicle access control against surface attack risk are standard planning requirements rather than optional upgrades. RAAS licensing under the Ministry of Public Security provides the regulatory framework; ex-IDF and ISA-background operators provide the operational standard.

Israeli RAAS regulatory compliance

Tel Aviv event security companies hold RAAS company licences; individual personnel hold RAAS personal licences with RAAS firearms permits for armed personnel. Israel Police co-ordination is confirmed for events above the statutory notification threshold.

item.title: Event Security in Vienna
item.description:
Vienna event security operates in a unique European context: a DSN High terrorism threat post-2020, a concentration of international organisations generating significant intelligence service activity, and a calendar of diplomatic and cultural events at venues including the Hofburg, Staatsoper, and UN Vienna International Centre. GewO Section 130 and WKO licensing provide the regulatory framework; coordination with UN UNDSS and Austrian Bundespolizei Wien is standard for the city’s highest-profile events.

Austrian GewO regulatory compliance

Vienna event security companies hold Gewerbeberechtigung under GewO Section 130. Individual personnel hold WKO Grundausbildung qualifications; CPOs hold additional Personenschutz credentials. WKO register confirms company status.

item.title: Event Security in Washington DC
item.description:
Washington DC event security serves the government-adjacent, diplomatic, and corporate policy sector event calendar in a city where Congressional building access protocols, federal facility security co-ordination, and counter-intelligence awareness are standard planning components. Tri-state licensing across DC, Maryland, and Virginia provides the regulatory framework; political environment monitoring and civil disorder planning are the DC-specific event security considerations.

Tri-state regulatory compliance

DC event security companies hold DC MPDC, Maryland State Police, and Virginia DCJS licences. Individual personnel hold state licences for their deployment jurisdiction. Multi-state compliance confirmed before each deployment.

item.title: Event Security in Zurich
item.description:
Zurich event security serves the financial capital’s concentrated banking, asset management, and insurance sector event calendar, alongside cultural events at the Opera House, Kunsthaus, and Art Basel Zurich. Swiss cantonal regulation at the Kantonspolizei Zurich level, with VSBU certification for individual security personnel, provides the regulatory framework; counter-intelligence awareness and HNWI luxury targeting at event departures are the primary security considerations specific to Zurich’s event environment.

Swiss cantonal regulatory compliance

Zurich event security companies hold Kantonspolizei Zurich registration. Individual security personnel hold VSBU-certified training at the appropriate level. Both are confirmed before deployment.

item.title: Event Security in Abu Dhabi
item.description: Abu Dhabi is a major destination for energy sector conferences, sovereign wealth and financial summits, and international defence exhibitions. The emirate’s event security industry operates under Federal Law No. 37 of 2006 on Private Security Services, with the Ministry of Interior (MoI) responsible for licensing security companies and approving personnel qualifications. Abu Dhabi Police provides strong local coordination and has established liaison protocols for major events at ADNEC and Yas Island.

item.title: Event Security in Amsterdam
item.description: Amsterdam is a major European conference and events destination, hosting international trade shows at RAI Amsterdam, financial and tech sector gatherings at Zuidas business district venues, and cultural events at historic locations including the Beurs van Berlage. The Dutch private security sector operates under the Wet particuliere beveiligingsorganisaties en recherchebureaus (WPBR, 1997), which requires security companies to hold police-granted permits and ensures personnel are screened and registered. The NCTV terrorism threat assessment at level 4 (Substantial) as of 2024 makes counter-terrorism planning a standard rather than exceptional component of event security planning in the Netherlands.

item.title: Event Security in Beijing
item.description:
Beijing is China’s political and economic capital, hosting a significant calendar of international corporate conferences, government-adjacent investor meetings, and diplomatic functions. Event security in Beijing operates under the Regulations on Administration of Security Services (2009), with the Public Security Bureau (PSB) responsible for licensing both security companies and individual security personnel. This regulatory framework is well-established, and Beijing’s own public safety infrastructure is extensive and capable.

The security context

Beijing’s ambient crime rate is low by the standards of major global cities, and the city has a well-resourced public security presence. Physical threat at standard corporate events is not the primary security concern. The relevant considerations for event security planners are different: the need to coordinate correctly with authorities when events attract diplomatic or government-adjacent attendance, the management of competitive intelligence risks at technology, energy, and investment-sector conferences, and the requirement that all security operators hold current PSB licences and Security Service Certificates under the 2009 Regulations.

item.title: Event Security in Berlin
item.description: Berlin is Germany’s primary city for large-scale trade fairs, international conferences, and political-adjacent corporate events. The security industry operates under Gewerbeordnung Section 34a (GewO §34a), which sets qualification requirements for security personnel, with the IHK Sachkundeprüfung as the standard for supervisory and close protection roles. The city’s event security landscape is shaped by two factors: an active political protest culture and a terrorism threat context that the BfV (Bundesamt für Verfassungsschutz) assesses as elevated across both jihadist and far-right strands.

item.title: Event Security in Cape Town
item.description:
Cape Town is South Africa’s premier international conference destination, hosting major events at the Cape Town International Convention Centre (CTICC), Waterfront hotel venues, and private estate locations across the Winelands. Event security operates within the framework established by the Private Security Industry Regulation Act 56 of 2001, with PSIRA (Private Security Industry Regulatory Authority) responsible for registering and regulating all commercial security personnel and companies.

The security context

The UK FCDO rates South Africa overall as high risk for crime, and Cape Town’s profile reflects this at a city level. The city contains areas of significant socioeconomic disparity; the Cape Flats has specific elevated crime indicators, while the central CBD, Waterfront, and Green Point areas where most major conference venues sit have more manageable risk profiles. The primary security concern for corporate events in Cape Town is opportunistic crime targeting wealthy attendees rather than terrorism or political violence. International principals arriving for conferences at CTICC or Waterfront hotels are visible high-value targets in the local environment, making close protection a routine operational consideration.

item.title: Event Security in Doha
item.description: Doha is a significant destination for energy sector conferences, sovereign wealth gatherings, and international diplomatic events. Qatar is the world’s largest LNG exporter by volume, and its event calendar reflects this: QNCC, West Bay hotel venues, and Katara Cultural Village regularly host events that draw CEO-level energy principals, government ministers, and international investors. Private security in Qatar operates under Law No. 8 of 2002 on Private Protective Security Services, with the Ministry of Interior responsible for licensing companies and personnel.

item.title: Event Security in Hong Kong
item.description: Hong Kong is one of Asia’s premier financial and conference destinations, drawing a high concentration of UHNWI clients, regional executives, and international investor principals. The city’s event security industry is governed by the Security and Guarding Services Industry Authority (SGSIA) under the Security and Guarding Services Ordinance Cap. 460, which sets clear licensing standards for commercial security operators. This regulatory framework, combined with a capable Hong Kong Police Force response infrastructure, provides a strong foundation for corporate event security.

item.title: Event Security in Kuala Lumpur
item.description: Kuala Lumpur is the commercial and financial centre of Malaysia and one of ASEAN’s primary conference destinations. The Kuala Lumpur Convention Centre (KLCC) at the base of the Petronas Twin Towers, MATRADE Exhibition and Convention Centre, and major hotel venues including the Mandarin Oriental and Shangri-La host a significant calendar of regional investment forums, energy sector events, and international corporate conferences. Private security companies operate under the Private Security Industry Act 2007 (Act 661), with Ministry of Home Affairs (MOHA) registration required for both companies and individual personnel.

item.title: Event Security in Madrid
item.description: Madrid is Spain’s primary city for major trade fairs, international conferences, and political-adjacent corporate events. IFEMA Madrid at Campo de las Naciones hosts a full calendar of large-scale international events; Palacio Municipal de Congresos and hotel conference venues in Salamanca and Recoletos serve the corporate and government sector calendar. Private security in Spain operates under Ley 5/2014 de Seguridad Privada, with the Cuerpo Nacional de Policia (CNP) responsible for registering and regulating all security companies and personnel.

item.title: Event Security in Toronto
item.description: Toronto is Canada’s largest city and primary corporate events destination, hosting major financial sector conferences at the Metro Toronto Convention Centre, international entertainment industry events during TIFF, and private functions at Bloor-Yorkville and Financial District hotel venues. Event security operates under the Private Security and Investigative Services Act, 2005 (PSISA), with the Ministry of the Solicitor General issuing Security Guard licences to individuals and Temporary Help Agency licences to companies. The Toronto Police Service has well-developed protocols for major event security liaison.

item.title: Event Security in Bangkok
item.description:
Bangkok is one of Asia’s primary corporate event destinations: the regional hub for Southeast Asian business, a conference city for financial services, technology, and real estate sectors. The security environment is less extreme than other cities on this platform but has specific characteristics that require active planning.

The security context

Bangkok’s terrorism risk is lower than Istanbul, Nairobi, or Mexico City. The city’s last significant terrorist attack was the 2015 Erawan shrine bombing, which killed 20 people at a tourist location. Subsequent attacks have been smaller. This is not a low-risk terrorism environment but the frequency of mass-casualty incidents is lower than in several other cities where major conferences are held.

item.title: Event Security in Bogota
item.description:
Bogota has undergone a genuine security transformation since the FARC peace process. The city that was effectively off the international conference circuit 20 years ago now hosts investment forums, technology conferences, and regional summits. The security environment is manageable. It is not low risk.

The current environment

Criminal groups including dissident FARC factions and urban criminal organisations maintain active kidnapping and extortion capability in Colombia. Bogota’s crime rate, while improved, remains elevated by international standards. The risk to corporate events is primarily from opportunistic targeting of identifiable high-value individuals rather than mass-casualty attacks.

item.title: Event Security in Brisbane
item.description:
Brisbane is Queensland’s capital and South East Queensland’s primary corporate event destination, supported by the BCEC at South Bank, the Brisbane Showgrounds precinct, and a strong city hotel infrastructure. The city’s selection as host of the 2032 Olympic Games has driven sustained investment in events infrastructure and has positioned Brisbane as a significant international conference destination alongside Sydney and Melbourne.

The security profile

QPS Crime Statistics 2024 confirm Brisbane as one of Australia’s lower-risk major cities for event security. The practical risk profile for corporate event planners centres on professional planning disciplines rather than active threat environments: access control for large delegate numbers, transfer management for arriving and departing executives, and close protection for high-profile speakers. The South Bank precinct, home to the BCEC, is a well-managed public-private environment with established security infrastructure and good co-operation from venue teams.

item.title: Event Security in Busan
item.description:
Busan is South Korea’s second-largest city and the country’s principal maritime gateway, home to one of the world’s largest container ports. The city hosts significant international events across the maritime, logistics, film, and technology sectors, with BEXCO in the Haeundae district serving as the primary convention and exhibition infrastructure. The Busan International Film Festival (BIFF) in October brings global media attention to the city annually.

The security profile

National Police Agency of Korea Crime Statistics 2024 confirm South Korea as a low general-crime environment. For corporate event planners, the security considerations in Busan are shaped by the international character of the events the city hosts rather than by ambient crime. Senior executives from major global shipping, port logistics, and maritime companies travel with commercially sensitive schedules and, for some, significant public profiles. The maritime sector’s concentration of commercially sensitive cargo and trade information adds a specific dimension to access control planning for maritime industry events.

item.title: Event Security in Calgary
item.description:
Calgary is the commercial capital of Canada’s oil and gas sector and one of the country’s primary corporate event destinations for energy industry conferences, investor days, and executive summits. The city hosts major events including the Global Energy Show and significant sector-specific investment conferences throughout the year. The BMO Centre expansion at Stampede Park and the Calgary TELUS Convention Centre in the CBD provide the primary large-format event infrastructure.

The security profile

Calgary Police Service Crime Statistics 2024 place Calgary among Canada’s lower-risk major cities for event security. The security considerations that matter for corporate event planning in Calgary are driven by the nature of the events the city hosts rather than by elevated ambient crime. Energy sector executives from major international oil, gas, and renewables companies travel with commercially sensitive schedules, significant shareholder scrutiny, and, in some cases, specific attention from activist campaigns. Close protection and executive transfer management are a routine element of security planning for this client profile.

item.title: Event Security in Dallas
item.description:
Dallas is one of the United States’ primary corporate event destinations, supported by the Kay Bailey Hutchison Convention Center, a strong hotel infrastructure in Uptown and the Arts District, and direct international air access via DFW and Love Field. The city hosts major trade shows, energy sector conferences, and technology summits throughout the year.

The security profile

The DPD Annual Crime Report 2024 identifies vehicle-related crime as a consistent concern in Dallas, particularly on major transit corridors including the DFW airport route. For corporate event planners, the operational risks that matter are transfer security between venues and hotels, access control at large-format or multi-building venues, and close protection for executives and keynote speakers who may attract public attention.

item.title: Event Security in Denver
item.description: Denver is the capital of Colorado and the commercial hub of the Rocky Mountain region, hosting major corporate events across the energy, technology, and agriculture sectors. The Colorado Convention Center in the downtown Arts District is one of the largest convention facilities in the western United States, and the city also hosts significant events at the Denver Performing Arts Complex and in the lower downtown (LoDo) district. Denver sits at 1,609 metres above sea level, the highest elevation of any major US city.

item.title: Event Security in Dubai
item.description:
Dubai hosts some of the world’s largest corporate events: GITEX, Arabian Travel Market, Dubai Airshow, Cityscape Global. The events infrastructure is excellent. The physical security risk is low. But event security in Dubai is not a checkbox exercise.

The specific risks at Dubai events

The primary security considerations at Dubai events are not criminal threats. They are reputational and legal. High-profile executives attending investment forums face business intelligence gathering by competing interests. Communications at sensitive boardroom events may be monitored. International guests unfamiliar with UAE law can inadvertently create legal liability for the event organiser.

item.title: Event Security in Glasgow
item.description:
Glasgow is Scotland’s largest city and the UK’s leading live music and events destination outside London. The Scottish Event Campus on the Clydeside is one of Europe’s busiest purpose-built events complexes, hosting major international conferences, trade shows, and concerts at the SEC Centre, SEC Armadillo, and OVO Hydro. The city also hosted COP26 in 2021 and has established infrastructure and experience for large-scale international events with significant security requirements.

The security profile

Police Scotland Crime Statistics 2024 confirm Glasgow as a well-managed city for major events by UK standards. The terrorism threat level assessed by the UK Home Office is Substantial as of 2026, which is the background security environment for all major public events in the UK, including those in Glasgow. This does not mean that Glasgow events face specific elevated threats, but it does mean that event security planning in the UK appropriately incorporates counter-terrorism awareness protocols for large gatherings, external event areas, and VIP movements.

item.title: Event Security in Istanbul
item.description:
Istanbul is a major European-Asian conference city: the gateway between Europe and the Middle East for investment, energy, and policy events. It is also on the FCDO SEVERE terrorism advisory.

The security environment

Turkey faces active terrorist threats from PKK/TAK (Kurdistan Workers’ Party affiliate) and residual ISIS capability. Istanbul has been hit by both. The Reina nightclub attack in 2017 killed 39 people at a location that was hosting a New Year’s event. The Ataturk Airport attack in 2016 killed 45. These are not historical footnotes; they inform current security planning.

item.title: Event Security in Johannesburg
item.description:
Johannesburg is sub-Saharan Africa’s largest economy by GDP and one of the continent’s primary conference destinations. The Sandton convention district competes with Dubai and Singapore for large-format corporate events. The security environment requires direct engagement rather than reassurance.

The security profile

South Africa’s violent crime statistics are among the most cited in the world. For corporate event planning, the specific risks that matter are carjacking, robbery during transfers, and residential/venue crime for events hosted outside hotel infrastructure. Johannesburg’s CBD and Sandton have different risk profiles: events in Sandton benefit from higher private security density and better infrastructure; events in the CBD require more detailed planning.

item.title: Event Security in Lagos
item.description:
Lagos is Nigeria’s commercial capital: home to the Nigerian Stock Exchange, the headquarters of most multinational Nigeria operations, and the country’s major port and logistics hub. It is also a city where corporate event security requires substantive planning rather than a nominal security presence.

The security environment

Kidnapping of business executives and high-net-worth individuals is a documented and active threat in Lagos. The frequency has varied over time, but the risk class is real and insurance underwriters treat it as such. For international executives attending Lagos events, this is the primary threat requiring specific mitigation.

item.title: Event Security in London
item.description:
London is one of the world’s leading corporate event cities: home to a professional-grade events infrastructure, an established private security industry, and a regulatory framework that is among the most developed globally.

The security environment

The UK terrorism threat level is SEVERE, meaning an attack is judged highly likely. London has experienced multiple significant attacks: Westminster Bridge (2017), London Bridge and Borough Market (2017), Manchester Arena (2017). The current threat comes primarily from Islamist extremism and lone actor attacks. This is the backdrop against which London events are planned.

item.title: Event Security in Mexico City
item.description:
Mexico City is Latin America’s largest metropolitan economy and a major conference destination for business, technology, and policy events. It operates at a different point on the risk spectrum from conference cities in Europe or East Asia.

The security environment

Mexico City’s risk profile is driven by organised crime rather than terrorism or civil conflict. Kidnapping, extortion, and vehicle crime are the primary security considerations for corporate event planners. The city’s police capability is uneven. Response times in an emergency are not comparable to London or Singapore.

item.title: Event Security in Moscow
item.description:
Moscow is included in this platform because corporate event enquiries continue to arrive from organisations with Russia operations, despite the current advisory environment. The honest and accurate assessment is that corporate event planning in Moscow for Western-connected organisations is a high-risk activity at present.

The current advisory position

FCDO advises against all travel to Russia. The State Department advises against all travel to Russia. Most European governments advise against all travel. These advisories exist for specific documented reasons: the war in Ukraine, the risk of detention of Western nationals, sanctions enforcement risk, and the general deterioration in the rule of law environment for foreign businesses.

item.title: Event Security in Mumbai
item.description:
Mumbai is India’s financial capital and the primary destination for international investor conferences, financial services events, and corporate summits. It is a sophisticated, complex city with a security environment that rewards specific planning rather than generic reassurance.

The security context

The 2008 attacks on the Taj Mahal Palace hotel, Oberoi Trident, and Chhatrapati Shivaji Terminus killed 166 people. Mumbai’s corporate conference circuit includes multiple hotels that were directly affected. Since 2008, the city’s major event venues have made substantive security investments. The quality of those investments varies.

item.title: Event Security in Nairobi
item.description:
Nairobi is the economic hub of East Africa and a major conference city for development finance, technology, and regional policy. It is also one of the more complex security environments on this platform.

The security context

Al-Shabaab has demonstrated both the intent and capability to conduct attacks in Nairobi targeting locations associated with international visitors and Western interests. The DusitD2 attack in 2019 killed 21 people at a complex that included offices, a hotel, and a conference facility. Event security planning in Nairobi begins with this baseline.

item.title: Event Security in New York
item.description:
New York City is one of the world’s primary corporate event destinations: the location of choice for investor summits, financial sector conferences, and high-profile product launches. The city has an exceptional private security industry and one of the most capable police counter-terrorism operations in the world.

The security context

New York has been a persistent target for terrorism: the 1993 World Trade Center bombing, the September 11 attacks, the 2017 truck attack on the Hudson River bike path, multiple disrupted plots. The NYPD Counter Terrorism Bureau has a sophisticated operational capability. The threat is real; the response infrastructure is also real.

item.title: Event Security in Paris
item.description:
Paris is one of Europe’s most significant conference cities: home to major international summits, technology events (VivaTech), luxury and fashion events, and political conferences at EU and NATO level. It is also one of the highest terrorism risk cities in Europe.

The security context

France has experienced some of Europe’s most severe terrorist attacks in the past decade: the Charlie Hebdo/Hyper Cacher attacks (January 2015, 17 dead), the Bataclan concert hall attack as part of the coordinated November 2015 attacks (130 dead), the Nice Bastille Day truck attack (86 dead, 2016). France’s counter-terrorism capability has been substantially enhanced since 2015. The Urgence Attentat alert level reflects the sustained nature of the threat.

item.title: Event Security in Perth
item.description: Perth is Western Australia’s capital and the administrative and commercial centre of Australia’s resources and mining economy. The city hosts a significant volume of corporate events tied to the resources sector, including investor days, executive summits, and major industry conferences such as the Diggers and Dealers Mining Forum held annually in Kalgoorlie. The Perth Convention and Exhibition Centre and Crown Perth entertainment complex are the city’s primary corporate event infrastructure.

item.title: Event Security in Philadelphia
item.description:
Philadelphia is one of the United States’ major convention cities, supported by the Pennsylvania Convention Center in Center City and a strong hotel infrastructure along Market Street and Broad Street. The city is also a significant life sciences and medical research hub, with University City hosting major academic medical centres and pharmaceutical industry conferences.

The security profile

The PPD Annual Report 2024 identifies vehicle crime in Center City as a consistent concern, with patterns that inform our transfer route selection for airport and inter-venue movements. Philadelphia’s convention infrastructure is well established and venue security teams at the Pennsylvania Convention Center are experienced, but the surrounding street environment in some blocks requires active management rather than passive trust in venue perimeter controls.

item.title: Event Security in Porto
item.description:
Porto is Portugal’s second city and the country’s northern business and cultural capital. The city is an established European conference destination, supported by Exponor in Matosinhos, the Alfandega Porto Congress Centre on the Douro waterfront, and a growing luxury hotel conference infrastructure. Porto is also the commercial gateway to the Douro Valley wine region and hosts significant international trade events in the wine, tourism, and technology sectors.

The security profile

PSP Crime Statistics 2024 confirm Porto as a low-crime city by European standards. The crime that most directly affects international business visitors is opportunistic theft, particularly pickpocketing in the historic Ribeira district and along the main Aliados boulevard where tourist density is high. For corporate event planners, this is primarily a delegate awareness and movement management consideration rather than a structural security threat, but it informs our transfer routing and delegate briefing protocols.

item.title: Event Security in Riyadh
item.description:
Riyadh is hosting an unprecedented volume of corporate and government events as Vision 2030 reshapes the Kingdom’s economic and social landscape. NEOM briefings, F1 Grand Prix hospitality, major financial sector conferences, and international government-level meetings have brought a significant new wave of event planning into Riyadh. The security planning for these events requires understanding the specific Riyadh risk picture rather than importing a generic Middle East template.

The security environment for events in Riyadh

Violent crime against foreign delegates at corporate events in Riyadh is not the primary planning concern. Saudi Arabia’s security forces are effective and the physical crime rate in the areas where international events are held is low. The risks that matter for event security planning in Riyadh are different from most other cities on this site.

item.title: Event Security in Sao Paulo
item.description: Sao Paulo is Brazil’s commercial capital and the primary venue for major corporate conferences, product launches, and private events in Latin America’s largest economy. The city has well-developed events infrastructure, including the WTC Sao Paulo, Transamerica Expo Center, and a concentration of five-star hotels in the Faria Lima and Jardins districts. The security planning for events in Sao Paulo is primarily about managing the transport risk around the event rather than the venue risk itself.

item.title: Event Security in Seattle
item.description:
Seattle is a major technology, aviation, and maritime industry hub and hosts a significant volume of corporate events including global technology product launches, investor conferences, and executive leadership summits. The Washington State Convention Center, currently undergoing expansion, and South Lake Union’s technology campuses are the city’s primary corporate event infrastructure.

The security profile

The SPD Crime Report 2024 notes elevated disorder in parts of downtown Seattle, particularly along the Third Avenue corridor, which has implications for event transfer routing. For corporate planners, the city’s risk profile is moderate: venue crime at established hotel and convention venues is low, but transfer security and street-level disruption require active management rather than standard transport logistics.

item.title: Event Security in Singapore
item.description:
Singapore is Southeast Asia’s primary corporate event hub: home to major financial services conferences, technology summits, and the regional headquarters of hundreds of multinationals. By most conventional security metrics it is one of the safest cities in the world.

The security environment

Singapore’s violent crime rate is extremely low. Terrorism plots have been disrupted but no mass-casualty attacks have occurred since the 1990s. The Internal Security Department (ISD) maintains a strong counter-terrorism capability. For most corporate event planners, Singapore’s security environment is not the primary concern.

item.title: Event Security in Sydney
item.description:
Sydney is Australia’s primary corporate event destination and a regional hub for Asia-Pacific business conferences, investment forums, and financial services events. The security environment is lower risk than most cities on this platform but not without specific threats.

The security context

ASIO maintains an Australia-wide terrorism threat level of PROBABLE. Sydney has experienced terrorist incidents: the 2014 Lindt Cafe siege (3 dead) was a watershed event for Australian security planning. The current primary threat is from lone actors inspired by Islamist extremism rather than organised group attacks. Australia’s counter-terrorism capability (ASIO, Australian Federal Police, NSW Police Counter Terrorism and Special Tactics Command) is advanced.

item.title: Event Security in Tokyo
item.description:
Tokyo is one of Asia’s most significant corporate event destinations. It hosts major technology conferences, automotive sector events, government-to-government forums, and investor roadshows for the world’s third-largest economy. By most security metrics it is one of the safest cities in the world.

The security environment

Tokyo’s violent crime rate is extremely low. Terrorism incidents are rare; the last mass-casualty attack (the 1995 sarin attack by Aum Shinrikyo) killed 13 people and is now three decades past. The National Police Agency has a capable counter-terrorism capability. Day-to-day criminal risk is minimal.
